prompt: Which Sanskrit dharmaśāstra, in its enumeration of the twelve kinds of witnesses, describes a category in which a man is hidden behind a wall and made to overhear a statement about a debt, so that — when the debt is later denied — he can come forward and report what he heard? Quote the verse that defines this concealed witness, and name the work and chapter.
rationale: The 'concealed by a wall, made to hear a statement about a debt' formulation of the gūḍha-sākṣin appears verbatim in Bṛhaspati's twelve-witness scheme; Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Kātyāyana etc. either lack this category entirely or do not phrase it with the wall-eavesdropper image.

prompt: Which Sanskrit legal treatise warns against forged copies of written instruments by comparing them to a reflection in a mirror — saying that just as a reflection appears real though it is unreal, so skilled persons make copies of documents? Quote the simile and identify the work and chapter section on documents.
rationale: The mirror-reflection simile for document forgery (Brh_1,6.33) is a distinctive Bṛhaspati image embedded in his elaborate treatment of lekhya, which goes well beyond the document-rules of Manu, Yājñavalkya or Nārada; no other smṛti in the corpus uses this metaphor.

prompt: Which ancient Indian work on judicial procedure anatomizes the court of justice as a 'ten-limbed instrument' in which the king is the head, the appointed judicial officer is the mouth, the assessors are the arms, the Smṛti is the hands, and the accountant and the scribe together form the shanks? Quote the verse listing these limbs and name the work and chapter.
rationale: The ten-limbed body-of-the-court metaphor — with the accountant and scribe explicitly assigned to the shanks, alongside the king-as-head and Smṛti-as-hands — is unique to Bṛhaspati's section on the court; no other corpus smṛti (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Yama, Viṣṇu, Aṅgirasa) anatomizes the court this way.
